text:
King's College Hospital 

Department of Neurosciences 

Clinic Date: 30 June 2022 

Dr Amelia Harding
Consultant Neurologist  
King's College Hospital  
Department of Neurosciences, King's College Hospital, London SE5 9RS  

Dear Dr Amelia Harding
Consultant Neurologist,  

Daniel Cooper, DOB: 14-03-1986, Hospital No: K742915 NHS No. 9075312468  
82 Brook Lane, London, SE15 3AB

Thank you for asking me to review your patient who was seen in follow-up in our epilepsy clinic. He attended with his partner, and we discussed his established diagnosis of focal epilepsy in the context of a remote history of mild traumatic brain injury sustained in his early twenties. He denies any other significant neurological illnesses. There is no known family history of epilepsy. 

He reports that his current seizures are typically preceded by a brief rising epigastric sensation and a metallic taste, followed by behavioural arrest, right-hand automatisms, and impaired awareness for approximately 1–2 minutes. Post-event, he is usually fatigued and disoriented for up to 30 minutes, with partial recall of the aura. There have been no witnessed generalised tonic–clonic seizures this calendar year. 

In order to document frequency clearly, he and his partner have kept a seizure diary, which aligns with our records: Focal seizure: 2022: Jan x1, Feb x1, Mar x2, Apr x2, May x1, Jun x1. They describe no clear nocturnal events and no injuries. Common precipitants appear to include sleep deprivation and missed meals. He does not drive and understands DVLA guidance. 

He is currently taking lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily with excellent adherence and no reported rash, diplopia, or mood change. He previously trialled levetiracetam, which was stopped due to irritability. There is no current alcohol or recreational drug use. Mood is stable; he denies anxiety or depressive symptoms, though he does note short-lived fatigue after seizures. 

Neurological examination today was normal, including cranial nerves, power, tone, coordination, and gait. No focal deficits were elicited. Prior MRI brain (2020, 3T epilepsy protocol) was reported as structurally normal. Routine EEG (2021) demonstrated left temporal interictal sharp activity. Blood tests from his GP in May 2022 including FBC, U&E, LFTs, and vitamin D were unremarkable. 

Synthesis: The clinical semiology, EEG findings, and diary collectively support a diagnosis of focal epilepsy, likely left temporal onset, in the setting of a remote mild TBI. Seizure control is fair with a low monthly frequency and preserved awareness of auras in many events. Given stability on lamotrigine and tolerability, I have recommended continuation of current therapy and ongoing seizure diary documentation. We discussed lifestyle measures including sleep hygiene, hydration, regular meals, and avoidance of missed doses. 

Plan: 
- Continue lamotrigine 200 mg twice daily. 
- Maintain seizure diary; if frequency increases or a generalised tonic–clonic seizure occurs, we will revisit treatment options (e.g., adjunctive therapy). 
- Arrange repeat routine EEG later this year to reassess interictal burden. 
- Safety counselling reinforced; he does not drive and will continue to follow DVLA regulations. 
- Follow-up in clinic in 6 months, or earlier if there is any deterioration. 

Please do not hesitate to contact me if you require any further information. 

Yours sincerely

entities.text: Focal seizure: 2022: Jan x1, Feb x1, Mar x2, Apr x2, May x1, Jun x1.

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: The letter provides explicit monthly counts for focal seizures in 2022 up to June: Jan 1, Feb 1, Mar 2, Apr 2, May 1, Jun 1. Summing Jan–Jun: 1 + 1 + 2 + 2 + 1 + 1 = 8 seizures over 6 months. No other seizure types with higher frequency are reported (no GTCs this year). According to the rules, select the highest frequency; here the only quantified frequency is 8 in 6 months. Normalize to allowed format: '8 per 6 month'.
